volumeTitle: The Little Carthaginian. Pseudolus. The Rope
label: The Rope (Section prologue.pr.1 - Section 1.2.143b)

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.1
sections.original: Qui gentes omnes mariaque et terras movet ,
sections.translation: [*] WITH him who sways all nations , seas , and lands , I am a fellow-citizen in the realms of the Gods . I am , as you see [*] , a bright and shining star , a Constellation that ever in its season rises

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.5
sections.original: hic atque in caelo : nomen Arcturo est mihi .
sections.translation: here on earth and in the heavens . Arcturus is my name . By night , I am glittering in the heavens and amid the Gods , passing among mortals in the day . Other Constellations , too , descend from the heavens upon the earth ; Jove , who is the ruler of Gods and men —

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.10
sections.original: is nos per gentis alium ália disparat ,
sections.translation: he disperses us here in various directions among the nations , to observe the actions , manners , piety , and faith of men , just as the means of each avail him . Those who commence villanous suits at law upon false testimony , and those who , in court , upon false oath deny a debt ,

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.15
sections.original: eorúm referimus nomina exscripta ad Iovem ;
sections.translation: their names written down , do we return to Jove . Each day does he learn who here is calling for vengeance . Whatever wicked men seek here to gain their cause through perjury , who succeed before the judge in their unjust demands , the same case adjudged does he judge over again ,

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.20
sections.original: maiore multa multat quam litem auferunt .
sections.translation: and he fines them in a penalty much greater than the results of the judgment they have gained . The good men written down on other tablets [*] does he keep . And still these wicked persons entertain a notion of theirs , that they are able to appease Jupiter with gifts , with sacrifice ; both their labour and their cost they lose . This , for this reason , is so ,

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.25
sections.original: nihil éi acceptumst a periuris supplici ;
sections.translation: because no petition of the perjured is acceptable to Him . If any person that is supplicating the Deities is pious , he will more easily procure pardon for himself than he that is wicked . Therefore I do advise you this , you who are good and who pass your lives in piety and in virtue —

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.30
sections.original: retinete porro , post factum ut laetemini .
sections.translation: still persevere , that one day you may rejoice that so you did . Now , the reason for which I ’ ve come hither , I will disclose to you . First , then , Diphilus [*] has willed the name of this city to be Cyrene [*] . There ( pointing to the cottage ) dwells Daemones , in the country and in a cottage very close adjoining to the sea ,

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.35
sections.original: senex , qui húc Athenis exul venit , hau malus ;
sections.translation: an old gentleman who has come hither in exile from Athens , no unworthy man . And still , not for his bad deserts has he left his country , but while he was aiding others , meanwhile himself he embarrassed : a property honorably acquired he lost by his kindly ways . Long since , his daughter , then a little child , was lost ;

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.40
sections.original: eam dé praedone vir mercatur pessumus ,
sections.translation: a most villanous fellow bought her of the thief , and this Procurer [*] brought the maiden hither to Cyrene . A certain Athenian youth , a citizen of this city , beheld her as she was going home from the music-school . He begins to love her ; to the Procurer he comes ;

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.45
sections.original: minis triginta sibi puellam destinat
sections.translation: he purchases the damsel for himself at the price of thirty minae , and gives him earnest , and binds the Procurer with an oath . This Procurer , just as befitted him , did not value at one straw his word , or what , on oath , he had said to the young man . He had a guest , a fit match for himself , an old man of Sicily ,

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.50
sections.original: scelestus , Agrigentinus , urbis proditor ;
sections.translation: a rascal from Agrigentum [*] , a traitor to his native city ; this fellow began to extol the beauty of that maiden , and of the other damsels , too , that were belonging to him . On this he began to persuade the Procurer to go together with himself to Sicily ; he said that there the men were given to pleasure ;

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.55
sections.original: dicit , potesse íbi eum fieri divitem .
sections.translation: that there he might be enabled to become a wealthy man ; that there was the greatest profit from courtesans . He prevails . A ship is hired by stealth . Whatever he has , by night the Procurer carries it on board ship from his house ; the young man who purchased the damsel of him

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.60
sections.original: ait sése Veneri velle votum solvere
sections.translation: he has told that he is desirous of performing a vow to Venus . This is the Temple of Venus , here ( pointing at it ) , and here , for that reason , has he invited the youth hither to a breakfast [*] . From there at once did he embark on board ship , and he carried off the courtesans . Some other persons informed the young man what things were going on ,

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.65
sections.original: lenonem abiisse . ad portum ádulescens venit :
sections.translation: how that the Procurer had departed . When the young man came to the harbour , their ship had got a great way out to sea . When I beheld how that the maiden was being carried off , I brought at the same instant both relief to her and destruction to the Procurer ; the storm I rebuked , and the waves of the sea I aroused .

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.70
sections.original: nam Arcturus signum sum omniúm acerrimum :
sections.translation: For the most violent Constellation of them all am I , Arcturus ; turbulent I am when rising , when I set , more turbulent still . Now , cast ashore there , both the Procurer and his guest are sitting upon a rock ; their ship is dashed to pieces . But this maiden , and another as well , her attendant ,

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.75
sections.original: de navi timidae desuluerunt in scapham .
sections.translation: affrighted , have leaped from the ship into a boat . At this moment the waves are bringing them from the rocks to land , to the cottage of this old man , who is living here in exile , whose roof and tiles the storm has stript off . And this is his servant who is coming out of doors .

sections.label: Section prologue.pr.80
sections.original: adulescens huc iam adveniet , quem videbitis ,
sections.translation: The youth will be here just now , and you shall see him , who purchased the maiden of the Procurer . Now , fare ye well , and may your foes [*] distrust themselves .

sections.label: Section 1.1.83
sections.original: Pro di immortales , tempestatem cuius modi
sections.translation: O ye immortal Gods , what a dreadful tempest has Neptune sent us this last night !

sections.label: Section 1.1.85
sections.original: detexit ventus villam — quid verbis opust ?
sections.translation: The storm has unroofed the cottage . What need of words is there ? It was no storm , but what Alcmena met with in Euripides [*] ; it has so knocked all the tiles from off the roof ; more light has it given us , and has added to our windows .

sections.label: Section 1.2.89
sections.original: Et vos a vestris abduxi negotiis
sections.translation: I have both withdrawn you from your avocations ,

sections.label: Section 1.2.90
sections.original: neque id processit qua vos duxi gratia ,
sections.translation: and that has not succeeded on account of which I ’ ve brought you ; I could not catch the Procurer down at the harbour . But I have been unwilling to abandon all hope by reason of my remissness ; on that account , my friends , have I the longer detained you . Now hither to the Temple of Venus am I come to see ,

sections.label: Section 1.2.95
sections.original: ubi rem divinam se facturum dixerat .
sections.translation: where he was saying that he was about to perform a sacrifice .

sections.label: Section 1.2.96
sections.original: Si sapiam , hoc quod me mactat concinnem lutum .
sections.translation: If I am wise , I shall be getting ready this clay that is awaiting me .

sections.label: Section 1.2.97
sections.original: Prope me hic nescio quis loquitur .
sections.translation: Some one , I know not who , is speaking near to me .

sections.label: Section 1.2.97b
sections.original: Heus , Sceparnio .
sections.translation: Hallo ! Sceparnio !

sections.label: Section 1.2.98
sections.original: Qui nominat me ?
sections.translation: Who ’ s calling me by name ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.98b
sections.original: Qui pro te argentum dedit .
sections.translation: He who paid his money for you .

sections.label: Section 1.2.99
sections.original: Quasi me tuom esse servom dicas , Daemones .
sections.translation: As though you would say , Daemones , that I am your slave .

sections.label: Section 1.2.100
sections.original: Luto usust multo , multam terram confode .
sections.translation: There ’ s occasion for plenty of clay [*] , therefore dig up plenty of earth . I find that the whole of my cottage must be covered ; for now it ’ s shining through it , more full of holes than a sieve .

sections.label: Section 1.2.103
sections.original: Pater salveto , ámboque adeo .
sections.translation: Health to you , good father , and to both of you , indeed .

sections.label: Section 1.2.103b
sections.original: Salvos sis .
sections.translation: Health to you .

sections.label: Section 1.2.104
sections.original: Sed utrum tu masne an femina es , qui illum patrem
sections.translation: But whether are you male or female , who are calling him father ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.105b
sections.original: Vir sum equidem .
sections.translation: Why really , I ’ m a man .

sections.label: Section 1.2.105c
sections.original: Quaere vir porro patrem .
sections.translation: Then , man , go seek a father elsewhere .

sections.label: Section 1.2.106
sections.original: Filiolam ego unam hábui , eam unam perdidi .
sections.translation: I once had an only daughter , that only one I lost . Of the male sex I never had a child .

sections.label: Section 1.2.107b
sections.original: At di dabunt .
sections.translation: But the Gods will give —

sections.label: Section 1.2.108
sections.original: Tibi quidem hercle , quisquis es , magnum malum ,
sections.translation: A heavy mischance to you indeed , I ’ faith , whoever you are , who are occupying us , already occupied , with your prating .

sections.label: Section 1.2.110
sections.original: Isticine vos habitatis ?
sections.translation: Pray are you dwelling there ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.110b
sections.original: Quid tu id quaeritas ?
sections.translation: Why do you ask that ? Are you reconnoitring the place for you to come and rob there ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.112
sections.original: Peculiosum esse addecet servom et probum ,
sections.translation: It befits a slave to be right rich in his savings , whom , in the presence of his master , the conversation cannot escape , or who is to speak rudely to a free man .

sections.label: Section 1.2.115
sections.original: Et impudicum et impudentem hominem addecet
sections.translation: And it befits a man to be shameless and impudent , for him to whom there ’ s nothing owing , of his own accord to come to the house of another person annoying people .

sections.label: Section 1.2.117b
sections.original: Tace , Sceparnio .
sections.translation: Sceparnio , hold your tongue .

sections.label: Section 1.2.118
sections.original: quid opust , adulescens ?
sections.translation: ( To PLESIDIPPUS .) What do you want , young man ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.118b
sections.original: Istic infortunium ,
sections.translation: A mishap to that fellow , who is in a hurry to be the first to speak when his master ’ s present .

sections.label: Section 1.2.120
sections.original: sed nisi molestumst , paucis percontarier
sections.translation: But , unless it ’ s troublesome , I wish to make enquiry of you in a few words .

sections.label: Section 1.2.121b
sections.original: Dabitur opera , atque in negotio .
sections.translation: My attention shall be given you , even though in the midst of business .

sections.label: Section 1.2.122
sections.original: Quin tu in paludem is exicasque harundinem ,
sections.translation: Rather , be off with you to the marsh , and cut down some reeds [*] , with which we may cover the cottage , while it is fine weather .

sections.label: Section 1.2.123b
sections.original: Tace .
sections.translation: Hold your tongue . Do you tell me ( to PLESIDIPPUS ) if you have need of anything .

sections.label: Section 1.2.124b
sections.original: Dic quod te rogo ,
sections.translation: Inform me on what I ask you ;

sections.label: Section 1.2.125
sections.original: ecquem tu hic hominem crispum , incanum videris ,
sections.translation: whether you have seen here any frizzle-headed fellow , with grey hair , a worthless , perjured , fawning knave .

sections.label: Section 1.2.126b
sections.original: Plurimos ,
sections.translation: Full many a one ; for by reason of fellows of that stamp am I living in misery .

sections.label: Section 1.2.128
sections.original: Hic dico , in fanum Veneris qui mulierculas
sections.translation: Him , I mean , who brought with him to the Temple of Venus here two young women , and who was to make preparations for himself

sections.label: Section 1.2.130
sections.original: ut rem divinam faciat , aut hodie aut heri .
sections.translation: to perform a sacrifice either to-day or yesterday .

sections.label: Section 1.2.131
sections.original: Non hercle , adulescens , iam hos dies complusculos
sections.translation: By my faith , young man , for these very many days past I haven ’ t seen any one sacrificing there ; and yet it can ’ t be unknown to me if any one does sacrifice there . They are always asking here for water , or for fire , or for vessels , or for a knife , or for a spit ,

sections.label: Section 1.2.135
sections.original: aut aulam extarem , aut aliquid — quid verbis opust ?
sections.translation: or for a pot for cooking [*] , or something or other . What need is there of words ? I procured my vessels and my well , for the use of Venus , and not my own . There has now been a cessation of it for these many days past .

sections.label: Section 1.2.138
sections.original: Vt verba praehibes , me periisse praedicas .
sections.translation: According to the words you utter , you tell me I ’ m undone .

sections.label: Section 1.2.139
sections.original: Mea quidem hercle causa salvos sis licet .
sections.translation: Really , so far as I ’ m concerned , I ’ faith , you may be safe and sound .

sections.label: Section 1.2.140
sections.original: Heus tu , qui fana ventris causa circumis ,
sections.translation: Hark you , you that are roaming about Temples for the sake of your stomach , ’ twere better for you to order a breakfast to be got ready at home .

sections.label: Section 1.2.142
sections.original: Fortasse tu huc vocatus es ad prandium ,
sections.translation: Perhaps you ’ ve been invited here [*] to breakfast . He that invited you , hasn ’ t he come at all ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.143b
sections.original: Admodum .
sections.translation: ’ Tis the fact .
